bids.reasoning: Since this is the very beginning of the negotiation, I will start by offering my most preferred values across all issues to set a strong anchor. This strategy guarantees my maximum potential utility configuration of 1.0 and allows me to see the opponent's counteroffer before making any concessions.

The opponent's message uses an emotional appeal but their opening offer gives me very low utility across most issues, indicating I should maintain a strong position. Since it is still extremely early in the negotiation (t=0.02), I will hold firm on my most critical issues (issueE and issueB) while making minor concessions on issueA and issueD to show a good-faith willingness to negotiate and find common ground.

The negotiation is still in its very early stages (t=0.04) so there is ample time to negotiate toward a fair agreement. I noticed the opponent's emotional appeals mask offers that continually lower my total utility, so I must stay cautious and guard my high-priority issues (issueE, issueB, and issueD). To demonstrate genuine cooperation, I will concede completely finding common ground on issueC by adopting the opponent's preferred value (valueB) while maintaining strong positions on my most heavily weighted issues.

The opponent continues to bid aggressively in their favor despite their emotional appeals, offering me near-minimal utility. Since we are still very early in the negotiation (t=0.06), I will make a measured concession by reducing my demands on my lower-weighted issues. I will offer a compromise on issueA while matching their preferred value on issueC, but I will maintain my high-utility values on my most important issues (issueE, issueB, and issueD).

The current time fraction is very low (t=0.08), allowing us to maintain a high-utility threshold. The opponent has repeated almost the identical offer with the lowest possible utility for my heavily weighted issues, while sending repetitive emotional messages that I am disregarding as cheap talk. I will attempt to make a slight concession on issueB (a heavily weighted issue for me) and hold issueC at valueB (which the opponent seems to prefer) while preserving strong value in my top priority issues.

The current time is t=0.10, which is very early in the negotiation. The opponent's messages consist of emotional appeals and do not contain concrete compromises that align with my highest weighted issues. To show cooperation, I will concede slightly on issueB (from valueD down to valueB) while maintaining the opponent's preferred value for issueC and holding firm on my most critical issues (issueD and issueE) to maintain an overall utility near 0.80.

The time fraction is 0.12, which is still very early in the negotiation, allowing me to maintain a strong position. The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals but their bids show only very marginal concessions, so I will treat their messages as cheap talk. I will keep offering their preferred value on issueC (valueB) to show cooperation, while holding firm on my highest-weight issues (issueD and issueE) and testing a slightly different combination on issueA and issueB to find mutually acceptable ground.

The current time fraction (t=0.14) is still very early in the negotiation. The opponent is relying heavily on generic emotional appeals but has made a minor concession on issueD from valueA to valueC. I will match this concession spirit by moving from valueE to valueD on issueD, keeping my overall utility around 0.79. Since issueE is extremely important to me (30% weight), I will hold firm on valueE, but continue trying to find mutually agreeable tradeoffs on the remaining issues.

The negotiation is still early (t=0.16), leaving ample time to explore acceptable trades. I read the opponent's emotional appeals as a negotiation tactic, while focusing primarily on their offer history. I will concede to their request for `issueD: valueC` since it still yields reasonable utility for me, while maintaining strong values on my highest-weight issues (`issueE` and `issueB`).

The opponent continues to use non-binding, emotionally manipulative language while simultaneously reverting to worse offers for me on issueD (from valueC back to valueB). Since the time fraction is still early (t=0.18), I will maintain a relatively high utility target above 0.75. I am matching their previously shown willingness to accept valueC on issueD while holding firm on my most important issue, issueE.

I see from your last offer that you are willing to give me my top preference (valueE) on issueD, for which I am grateful. I will lock in valueB for issueC and valueE for issueD, acknowledging your bids on these issues. In return, I am holding firm on my priorities for issues B and E, as it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.20) and we can continue exploring trades that balance our core interests.

The opponent claims to have made a major concession on issueE, moving to valueC, but at the same time they retracted their excellent prior offer (valueE) on issueD. I will acknowledge their movement on issueE by making my first concession there (dropping from valueE to valueD), but I will insist on capturing the valueE they previously signaled was acceptable for issueD. Since time is still early (t=0.22), I want to maintain a high utility (>0.78) while matching their cooperative rhetoric with a meaningful shift on my most important issue.

The opponent is consistently offering valueA for issues A and B, and valueB for issue C, taking rigid positions while attempting to use emotional language to secure concessions. I will maintain my necessary utility on my highest stakes issues (E and B) by offering valueD for both. I will accommodate their recent offer on issueD by selecting valueD, and I will continue matching their clear preference on issueC (valueB). This represents a reasonable step in finding middle ground while keeping my utility close to 0.78 early in the negotiation.

The opponent's messages are purely emotional appeals ("open heart", "means so much") to mask low-utility offers, which I will treat as cheap talk. However, they have consistently offered `issueC: valueB` and often `issueD: valueD`, both of which provide acceptable utility for me (especially `valueD` for `issueD` at 0.98). I will anchor on these two points of agreement, offer `issueB: valueB` to explore concessions, and hold firm on my most important issue, `issueE: valueE`.

The current time fraction is only 0.28, allowing me to maintain high utility above 0.75. The opponent's messages are exclusively emotional appeals and generic persuasion tactics, which I treat as cheap talk; however, their actual bidding behavior consistently asks for `issueC: valueB` and shows some flexibility on `issueD` over time. I will accommodate their preference for `issueC: valueB` while proposing high-utility values for my three most heavily-weighted issues (`issueE`, `issueD`, and `issueB`) to continue probing for an equitable tradeoff.

The opponent's emotional appeals remain persistent but are purely cheap talk, so I will ignore them and focus strictly on their bidding patterns. They are consistently bidding `valueB` on `issueC` (which provides me a decent utility of 0.73), and recently explored `valueB` for `issueB` alongside `valueE` for `issueD` in previous turns. I will incorporate those flexible points to find mutual ground while holding firm on `issueE`, which carries my highest weight, to maximize my overall utility.

The opponent is consistently making emotional appeals, which I will treat as non-binding cheap talk. Their offers strongly favor `issueA: valueA` and `issueB: valueA`, while frequently proposing `issueC: valueB`. Since `issueC` is one of my lower-priority issues (0.1 weight) and `valueB` yields me decent utility (0.73), I can accommodate it. I will hold firm on my highest priority issues (`issueE`, `issueB`, and `issueD`), offering a bid with a total utility around 0.81, recognizing the early stage (t=0.32) allows for continued exploration of high-utility compromises.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive emotional appeals, which I will ignore in favor of their actual bidding behavior. In their latest turn, they conceded issueA to valueB. Since my weight for issueA is low (0.1), I will match this concession to show flexibility. To maintain high utility (~0.76) at this stage (t=0.34), I will continue to hold firm on my highest-weight issues (issueE and issueD) and offer valueB for issueC, which they have consistently proposed.

The opponent's messages consist of emotional appeals which I will treat as cheap talk, maintaining focus on the actual numerical exchanges. I will lock in issueC: valueB and issueD: valueE to demonstrate cooperation, while proposing issueE: valueD and issueB: valueD to keep my overall utility at a strong ~0.78, appropriately high for t=0.36.

I will adopt those values alongside their persistent request for `issueC: valueB`. I will retain a strong position on my highest-priority issues (issueE and issueD) while conceding slightly to a value they recently offered on issueB (valueB) to assemble a package that maintains my utility above 0.75 while acknowledging their flexibility.

I will propose a package that incorporates these previously offered values (issueB: valueB, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueE) while holding firm on my most important issue, issueE, and slightly modifying issueA to avoid a repetitive offer while keeping my utility safely above 0.75.

The opponent's numerous messages are completely emotional appeals with no factual constraints or new value discoveries, so I will treat them as cheap talk. Focusing on the numbers, the opponent repeatedly proposes valueA for issueA and valueB for issueC. Since both of these issues carry a low weight (0.1) for me, I am willing to maintain my concession on issueC to their preferred valueB while offering valueC on issueA. I will maintain high utility targets for my most important issues, issueE and issueD, aiming for a fair mid-game compromise that protects my overall utility.

The opponent's messages rely strictly on emotional pleading, but scanning their bid history shows they have consistently sought valueA for issueA and recently offered valueE for issueC. I am willing to concede towards their preferences on lower-weight issues (A and C) by adopting valueB for issueA and mirroring their valueE for issueC. I will combine this with valueB on issueB which they have previously extended, while holding firm on issues D and E where my weight is paramount to maintain a strong overall utility (~0.77).

I am approaching the midpoint of the negotiation (t=0.48). The opponent consistently relies on emotional appeals as cheap talk, yet their actual bids show a strong fixation on `issueA: valueA` and recent shifts to `issueB: valueB` and `issueC: valueD`. Since my weights on `issueA` and `issueC` are low, I will concede `issueA` to their preferred value and match their recent values for `issueB` and `issueC`. To balance these concessions and maintain my overall utility above 0.75, I will stand firm on my highest-weight issues, securing `valueD` for `issueD` (which they have offered previously) and holding my top value for `issueE`.

At t=0.5, I am targeting a utility of roughly 0.76. The opponent uses persistent emotional appeals but has consistently lowballed me on issue E (offering value A/B), which is my most important issue (weight 0.3). Therefore, I will hold absolutely firm on issue E by demanding value E. To compensate and find common ground, I will concede on issue A to value B (which they just offered) and adopt their recent offers for issue B (value B) and issue C (value D), while asking for issue D = value E (which they have previously demonstrated a willingness to accept).

The time is at 0.52, entering the mid-game phase. The opponent's messages consist of heavy emotional appeals, which I will treat as cheap talk. Interestingly, their recent offers incorporated `valueD` for both `issueB` and `issueC`. Both of these values actually provide me very high utility (0.82 and 0.87 respectively), so I will adopt them to show cooperation. I will hold firm on my highest-weight issues (`issueD` and `issueE`) to protect my overall utility score, keeping my total utility well above 0.8.

The opponent has sent several emotion-laden messages trying to persuade me to concede, which I treat as non-binding negotiation tactics. In their latest bid, they have offered values that are reasonably good for my utility on issues B, C, and D (valueB, valueD, and valueE respectively). To move us closer to agreement while the time fraction passes the halfway mark (0.54), I will match their exact offer on issues A, B, C, and D, but shift issueE to valueD to protect my priority on my highest-weighted issue.

They previously accepted `issueB: valueB`, and at t=0.54 they offered `issueD: valueE`, which is excellent for my utility. In their last turn, they offered `issueC: valueE`, which corresponds to my highest preference for issue C. I will lock in those favorable values along with their consistent demand for `issueA: valueA` and recent ask for `issueC: valueB`. I am seeing the opponent repeatedly deploying emotional appeals while returning to some core values on issues like issueA (`valueA`) and recently issueB (`valueB`) and issueC (`valueC`). I will concede to their exact requested values on issueA, issueB, and issueC in order to accommodate them and build goodwill. In exchange, I hold firm on issueD and issueE, ensuring a utility of roughly 0.73, which appropriately reflects our progression into the second half of the negotiation (t=0.6).

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive emotional appeals without concrete negotiation points, so I will treat them as cheap talk and rely entirely on the offer history. At t=0.58, the opponent offered a package containing `issueB: valueD`, `issueC: valueB`, and `issueE: valueE`, which yields high utility for me if combined properly. I will concede entirely on `issueA` to their preferred `valueA` while maintaining my top values on `issueD` and `issueE`, bringing my target utility to ~0.75, which is appropriate for t=0.62.
